coredata.dc:title: Fabrication, characterization and response surface method optimization for quantum efficiency of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots obtained from carboxymethylcellulose of oil palms empty fruit bunch
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Chemical Industry and Engineering Society of China, and Chemical Industry Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Chemical Industry and Engineering Society of China, and Chemical Industry Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdullah Issa, Mohammed
Abidin, Zurina Zainal
Sobri, Shafreeza
Abdul-Rashid, Suraya
Mahdi, Mohd Adzir
Ibrahim, Nor Azowa
Pudza, Musa Y.
coredata.dc:description: Bio based nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were obtained from empty fruit bunch carboxymethylcellulose and ethylenediamine (EDA) through one-pot hydrothermal carbonization route. The optimum as-formed N-CDs were thoroughly characterized via Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), UV–vis spectra (UV–Vis) and Fluorescence spectra (PL). Response surface methodology was statistically used to assess three independent variables that have major influence on the fluorescence quantum yield (QY), including temperature (230–270 °C), time (2–6 h) and EDA mass (10%–23.3%). Based on analysis of variance (ANOVA) results, synthesis temperature was found to be the most influential factor on the QY, followed by time and EDA mass. Higher temperature, long synthesis time and high amount of EDA were satisfactorily enough for efficient carbonization conversion rate and obtaining highest QY of N-CDs. The obtained quadratic model (R 2 = 0.9991) shows a good correlation between the experimental data and predicted values. The optimum synthetic parameters are of 270 °C temperature, 6 h reaction time and 23.3% of EDA mass. The optimized as-made N-CDs exhibited blue photoluminescence with both excitation dependent/independent phenomena and high nitrogen content. The maximum emission intensity was 426 nm at a maximum excitation wavelength of 320 nm, with a QY of up to 22.9%. XPS and FTIR data confirmed the existence of polar containing groups, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups over the surface of N-CDs whereas nitrogen species in the form of (pyridinic and graphitic −N) were introduced in the aromatic carbon domains, which imparts the hydrophilic and photostability of N-CDs. Taking into account the low-cost and sustainable production of N-CDs, this method considered a feasible route for converting low quality waste into value-added nanomaterials and utilizing for different functionalization processes and analytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Response surface methodology
Optimization
Nitrogen doped

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantum yield calculations for the production of N-CD
Interaction effect of variables on fluorescence efficiency of N-CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 The Chemical Industry and Engineering Society of China, and Chemical Industry Press. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Universiti Putra Malaysia for funding this project ( GP-IPS/2017/9556800 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagnostic plot for developed model adequacy showing a) actual response versus predicted response for QY and b) studentized residuals versus run number.

Response surface design of the QY versus (a) synthesis temperature and time, (b) temperature and EDA weight and (c) time and EDA mass. The third variable of each graph was kept constant at central point.
3D surface (a) and contour plot (b) of optimum condition for optimization of QY.
TEM images of the N-CDs in different magnifications (a, b), HRTEM image (c) and the histogram showing the distribution particle size (d).
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N-CDs, (b) high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s and (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s.

Optical properties of N-CDs showing absorption spectra (black line) combined with PL spectra (red line). The inset shows digital photos of N-CDs under daylight (left) and exposed to UV light at 365 nm (right).
PL spectra of N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–420 nm).
Schematic representation for the preparation of N-CDs from EFB carboxymethylcellulose and ethylenediamines.
Proposed mechanism for the formation on N-CDs.
Experimental and coded levels of significant independent variables
CCD design matrix for the three variables with the experimental values of QY
ANOVA results for response surface quadratic model

head.ce:title: Fabrication, characterization and response surface method optimization for quantum efficiency of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots obtained from carboxymethylcellulose of oil palms empty fruit bunch

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Computer & Communications Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 UPM Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Computer & Communications Systems Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bio based nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were obtained from empty fruit bunch carboxymethylcellulose and ethylenediamine (EDA) through one-pot hydrothermal carbonization route. The optimum as-formed N-CDs were thoroughly characterized via Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS), UV–vis spectra (UV–Vis) and Fluorescence spectra (PL). Response surface methodology was statistically used to assess three independent variables that have major influence on the fluorescence quantum yield (QY), including temperature (230–270 °C), time (2–6 h) and EDA mass (10%–23.3%). Based on analysis of variance (ANOVA) results, synthesis temperature was found to be the most influential factor on the QY, followed by time and EDA mass. Higher temperature, long synthesis time and high amount of EDA were satisfactorily enough for efficient carbonization conversion rate and obtaining highest QY of N-CDs. The obtained quadratic model (R 2 = 0.9991) shows a good correlation between the experimental data and predicted values. The optimum synthetic parameters are of 270 °C temperature, 6 h reaction time and 23.3% of EDA mass. The optimized as-made N-CDs exhibited blue photoluminescence with both excitation dependent/independent phenomena and high nitrogen content. The maximum emission intensity was 426 nm at a maximum excitation wavelength of 320 nm, with a QY of up to 22.9%. XPS and FTIR data confirmed the existence of polar containing groups, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups over the surface of N-CDs whereas nitrogen species in the form of (pyridinic and graphitic −N) were introduced in the aromatic carbon domains, which imparts the hydrophilic and photostability of N-CDs. Taking into account the low-cost and sustainable production of N-CDs, this method considered a feasible route for converting low quality waste into value-added nanomaterials and utilizing for different functionalization processes and analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new member of carbonaceous materials, carbon dots (CDs) have gained a considerable focusing due to their remarkable properties such us biocompatibility, nontoxicity, chemical and photostability, and simplicity of production [1]. These properties make them ideal for bioimaging, photocatalysis, drug delivery and chemical sensing [2].

Nowadays, many synthetic strategies, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, microwave assisted method and hydrothermal carbonization route have been extensively studied for the production of CDs. However, these methods are typically expensive, time-consuming processes and generally require harsh toxic chemicals. Therefore, finding a low-cost method with non-toxic precursors is still a hot topic area.

Over the last few years, different biomass-based materials, including the usage of starch, glucose and cellulose have been widely employed for the production of CDs. In spite of their sustainability, the fluorescent efficiency resulted from these natural resources is still low. This motivates the researchers for doping the carbon dots with various heteroatom agents since they effectively enhanced the fluorescence emission of the semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). Among heteroatoms, nitrogen doping agent has been found to be a convenient candidate for a wide range of functions as they remarkably enhance the fluorescence efficiency. However, a clear formation mechanism for nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) synthesized hydrothermally from saccharides and various heteroatom doping sources along with the origin of the PL emission are still under debate.

Up to now, there is a lack of optimization investigations on the response surface methodology (RSM) application in the CDs synthesis approach as most of the previous studies are still using conventional one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) methods [3–5]. However, the RSM has been proved to be a promising statistical tool, in terms of time-consumption and high expense of reagent and material utilization, for studying the influence of the vital synthetic variables on the response of interest and at the same time maximizing the response to meet the application requirements [6].

In this study, facile hydrothermal route of carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) derived from empty fruit bunch (EFB) of oil palms in the presence of ethylenediamine as both N-doping agent and surface passivation source has been used to obtain nitrogen-doped carbon dots. Response surface methodology incorporated with central composite design (RSM-CCD) was statistically used to study the relationship of three key operating variables, including reaction temperature, reaction time, and EDA mass on the quantum yield (QY), and the synthesis parameters were optimized. A model was generated via regressive examination and analysis of variance (ANOVA) technique. The numerically optimized N-CDs with the highest percentage of QY were thoroughly characterized to establish its properties. A systematic representation of the N-CDs formation mechanism based on the current findings was also proposed.

CMC from empty fruit bunch of oil palms was collected from Waris Nove Company, Malaysia. Ethylenediamine and quinine sulfate were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Deionized water (DI) was used through the whole research.

The details of the preparation procedure are described as follows: CMC was cut into small pieces using a home-use mixer grinder for 3 min. 0.3 g of fine CMC was dispersed in DI, and then mixed with a rational amount of EDA. The mixture was sonicated, transferred to a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor, sealed and heated at 270 °C for 6 h in an oven. After cooling down naturally, the resulted dark brown dispersion was centrifuged for 10 min and filtered using a membrane (0.22 μm) to get rid of larger particles and unreacted precursors.

The QY measurements of all the as-made N-CDs were calculated based on the comparative method [7–9]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol⋅L-1 H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm) was selected as reference fluorophore. The UV absorbance of all the samples was adjusted to obtain optical density below 0.05 at the corresponding excitation wavelength to decrease the inner filter effect that might distort the signal readout. The determination of QY was performed using the following formula:
(1)
QY =
Q R
I
I R
OD R
OD
n 2
n
R 2
where the subscript R represents values for quinine sulfate, QY is the quantum yield, I is the integrated area under the emission peak, OD is the optical density at 320 nm, and n is the refractive index. The ratio of n/n
R was kept to be 1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) readings were carried out using a Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope (acceleration voltage of 200 kV). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Thermo Nicolet FT-IR spectrometer of 4 cm−1 resolution) was measured using KBr as a standard in the range of 650 to 4000 cm−1. UV–Vis spectra were recorded with a spectral range of 200–800 nm using a Shimadzu UV-1800 Spectrophotometer and the fluorescence studies were measured in quartz cuvettes with 1 cm path length using an LS 55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) with a slit width of 15 nm for both excitation and emission and a scan rate of 250 nm·min−1. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) (Physical Electronics PHI 5400 spectrometer, Mimos Semiconductors, Malaysia) were measured using Al-Ka radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). Prior to de-convolution, charge modification was established at C1s by setting binding energies of CC and CH at 284.8 eV. Origin 9.0 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA) was used for PL curve fitting and Design-Expert 10.0 (Stat Ease Inc., Minneapolis, USA) was carried out for statistical analysis.

RSM is commonly applied with the incorporation of central composite design to study the effect of the operating parameters (input variables) that have the main influence on the response of interest (output variable) [10]. Based on several trial and error experiments, the main operating variables were chosen to be as follows: synthesis temperature (X 1), synthesis time (X 2), and EDA mass (X 3). A face-centered central composite design (FC-CCD) was employed according to the selected variables. The practical range and level of the factors identified were outlined in Table 1 . The codes of (−1) and (+1) indicate the low and high levels of the statistical experiment matrix, respectively.

For statistical analysis, Eq. (2) illustrates the coded dimensionless independent variables (X
i ) based on the terms of the observed independent factor (x
i ), observed independent center point factor (x 0), and the step change value (∆x
i ) [11].
(2)
X i
=
x i
−
x o
∆
x i

The response (Y) was then predicted according to Eq. (3) by the second-degree polynomial equation in terms of the constant coefficient (β 0), linear coefficients (β
i ), quadratic coefficients (β
ii ), interaction coefficients (β
ij ), and the residue (ε), where k is the number of variables included in the experiments [12].
(3)
Y =
β 0
+
∑
i = 1
k
β i
x i
+
∑ i k
β ii
x i 2
+
∑
1 ≤ i ≤ j
k
β ij
x i
x j
+ ε

The total number of experimental runs proposed by the Design-Expert software was 18 as presented in Table 2 , with 8 factorial point runs, 6 axial point runs and 4 center point runs as replicate for experimental error estimation. The mass of CMC and the ratio of EDA/water were fixed to be 0.3 g and 4, respectively, throughout all the designing experiments.

The assessment of RSM model was achieved using statistical analysis of central composite design. Normally, three main tests, including significant of terms, regression model and lack-of-fit test were used to assess the fitness and reliability of a model. Multiple polynomial models including Linear, 2FI (two factorial), quadratic, and cubic models were fitted based on the experimental data for obtaining the regression equation using analysis of variance (ANOVA). Probability values F-value and P-value were used to determine the most influential factor on the response of interest based on significance of terms. Higher F-value along with lower P-value (normally less than 0.05) of the model terms is considered to have vital influence on the response. The lack-of-fit test indicates the unsuccessful model to explain the data of experimental values that are excluded from the regression. The significant model should have non-significant lack-of-fit, which suggests that the residual error has not exceeded pure error and consequently the model is significant. To verify the adequacy of the model, determination coefficient (R 2), adjusted R 2, predicted R 2, and predicted error sum of squares (PRESS) are used. The value of R 2 represents the percentage of the total variations in experimentation which are not described by the model whereas adjusted R 2 is the variation of the mean presented by the model in which the number of variables of the data set is taken into account. Typically, a good model should have R 2 value of close to one, and this indicates that better fitness between actual data and the empirical model would be achieved. The reproducibility of the model was tested using the coefficient of variation (CV) and predicted error sum of squares (PRESS). Normally, the CV ≤10% and PRESS of greater than 4 is considered as an acceptable reproducibility for the model. The effect of individual variables and their interaction effect on the response were further investigated using three-dimension graphs. The suggested model was finally used to identify and validate the optimum conditions of each variable for obtaining highest percentage of quantum yield.

The hydrothermal carbonization route (HTC), which is a simple and green approach, was used for the production of N-CDs from EFB carboxymethylcellulose and ethylenediamines, as presented in Scheme 1 . After 6 h treatment at 270 °C, the color of the aqueous dispersion was changed to dark brown, indicating the formation of N-CDs [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield calculations for the production of N-CD
ce:section.ce:para: All the as-made N-CDs were characterized using UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra, respectively for obtaining the relative fluorescence QY of each experimental run. Taking Run #4 as an example, the absorption peak is observed at about (320 ± 3) nm — a typical peak for all N-CDs samples. The fluorescence peak at about (426 ± 3) nm for N-CDs solution was observed when excited at 320 nm. Similar PL emission also refers to all of the N-CDs samples. Prior to QY calculation, the emission curve has been integrated for obtaining the corrected area under the peak. The spectroscopic data illustrated in Table 3 were collected based the absorption and fluorescence corrected area under the peak and hence used to calculate the QY based on Eq. (1). Similar procedure of each run was used to calculate fluorescence QY based on the obtained spectroscopic data.

Table 4 shows the outcome of the sequential model sum of squares for the corresponding experimental matrix. It can be seen that quadratic model was suggested by the software as the recommended model for this design based on experimental data of the obtained QY whereas, the cubic model was considered to be aliased.

In order to ensure the sufficiency of the suggested model, an examination for significance and fitness of the quadratic model based on P-value and F-test was carried out as presented in Table 5 . The model was significant as confirmed by the P-value (<0.0001) and F-value (97.98). The results of ANOVA clearly show that the P-values of all three variables and their interactions, except for the interactions between time with both temperature and EDA mass and the quadratic effect of time, were found to be significant and hence will be involved in the final model. Among all the significant factors, temperature was found to be the most influential factor that has the major effect on the QY. A second order empirical model expressed in Eq. (2) in terms of the coded factor was attained according to ANOVA results.
(2)
QY = 15.85 + 5.13
X 1
+ 3.81
X 2
− 1.72
X 3
+ 0.13
X
1
X 2
+ 1.74
X 1
X 3
− 0.47
X 2
X 3
− 2.8
X 1 2
− 0.41
X 2 2
− 1.45
X 3 2

The reliability of the model was also assigned in terms of lack-of-fit test as presented in Table 5. The non-significant lack-of-fit (P-value = 0.168) confirms the significance of the model [14]. Furthermore, the precision of the model is evaluated by the coefficient of determination R 2. In this study, the R 2 value was 0.9991 confirming that only 0.9% of the entire response variation was not described by the model. The adjusted R 2 value of 0.9809 was in a reasonable agreement with R 2 value, indicating that the model has related variables. The reproducibility and fitting of the model were also assigned in terms of coefficient of variation (C. V. %) and the precision residual sum of square (PRESS), respectively (shown in Table 5). The CV of 5.88% confirms the efficient reproducibility of the model. Moreover, the healthy correlation between the experimental data and the predicted model is assumed when the PRESS is greater than 4. Therefore, the PRESS was acceptable for the suggested quadratic model.

It can be said that the quadratic model accurately explained the effect of the N-CDs synthesis conditions on the QY as both of the experimental QY and the QY values predicted by the ANOVA almost formed a straight line (Fig. 1a). In addition, since all the points in the residual plot of actual values were located within the acceptable range and were close to the center line (Fig. 1b), then the observed values for QY explanation have satisfactory residuals. In other words, the experimental data was satisfactorily fitted to the model and can be employed to assess, evaluate and predict the interactions among the significant variables for obtaining high fluorescence efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction effect of variables on fluorescence efficiency of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: According to ANOVA, the results of the obtained model were facilitated to study the effects of the synthesis factors and their interaction on QY of N-CDs in the form of three-dimensional (3D) response surface plot. Fig. 2a shows the effect of temperature and synthesis time on the QY at 16.7% of EDA mass. It can be clearly observed that both temperature and synthesis time were positively associated with the QY. In other words, in the synthesis condition in which the temperature and the synthesis time were low, only a polymerization reaction takes place and consequently the EFB carboxymethylcellulose was not completely carbonized. The findings provided evidence that when the reaction time is sufficient, the higher the temperature goes, the more efficient QY would be synthesized. The interaction effect of temperature and EDA weight on the QY at 4 h of reaction time is presented in Fig. 2b. The significance of EDA mass becomes obvious as the temperature increases to the highest. The higher impact of temperature on the QY can be explained by the fact that higher temperature can lead to complete carbonization process and hence higher QY can be obtained. The effect of reaction time and EDA mass on the QY at 250 °C synthesis temperature is illustrated in Fig. 2c. It can be summarized that both the time and EDA weight (up to 16.7 W/W) were found to be positively related to the QY. In other words, by increasing both parameters, higher QY can be obtained. This can be explained by the fact of doping effect. The existence of nitrogen bounds may disorder the hexagonal ring structure, introduce emissive trap states within the crystalline sp2 core and hence improve the fluorescence QY [15]. In short, the response surface analysis results showed that the synthesis temperature was the most influential factor for efficient carbonization conversion rate of N-CDs, followed by time and EDA mass. This result was consistent with the regression analysis results, as temperature had the highest regression coefficient.

The main function of the experimental design is to figure out the optimum HTC synthesis conditions where best percentage of QY can be determined from the carbonization conversion of CMC. Optimization of the synthesis factors was examined by numerical optimization method in the Design-Expert 10.0 software. The 3D response surface and contour plot at maximum synthesis region for optimum production of QY were presented in Fig. 3a and b. The optimized synthesis parameters were found to be 268.28 °C, 5.99 h and 23.13% of EDA mass, in which QY was predicted to be 23.12%. For operation convenience, the optimum operating parameters were selected as 270 °C, 6 h and 23.3% of EDA mass. The predicted and observed values for the QY production were found to be 23.27% and 22.99%, respectively. Thereby, it was concluded that the empirical model obtained from RSM experimental design, can accurately explain the relationship between the operating factors and the response.

In this study, CMC was used as the carbon source with the incorporation of EDA as both N-doping molecule and surface-passivating agent through one-pot HTC route. Up to now, there is no definite evidence to the possible chemical reactions occurring inside the HTC autoclave for the formation of N-CDs obtained from polysaccharides and various nitrogen doping sources. However, a simplified major step is proposed for the possible reaction pathway in the production of N-CDs as presented in Scheme (2) according to the literature reports of HTC of polysaccharides [16–23]. Firstly, at high temperature (T ≥ 270 °C), CMC molecules hydrolyze in aqueous solution to form glucose. At the same time, free amino groups in EDA react with the aldehyde of glucose molecule to form glucosamines. As a result, aminoketones or Amadori compounds are generated via dehydration and fragmentation reactions leading to the production of hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) via successive dehydration processes, which is considered the main reactive intermediate in HTC.

Water can act as strong solvent through long-time process leading to the rehydration reaction of HMF to produce water soluble organic acids such as levulinic acid and formic acid. Multiple processes, including fragmentation, substitution, aldol condensation, reversion, and/or successive dehydration reactions may take place to transform these bi-products into various soluble polymeric products. It is believed that the existence of aldehyde and alcohol groups in HMF may accelerate the polymerization process to form conjugated networks. Based on the concepts of the LaMer model, the aromatization and carbonization take place by nuclear growth of these aromatic groups at a critical concentration at the supersaturation point (Scheme 2). Different polar oxygenated groups, including the formation of carbonyl and dicarbonyl groups, were introduced to the surface of carbon dots as a result of oxidative decomposition of Amadori products. At the same time, most amines in EDA were doped into the crystalline structure of sp2 carbon framework to form pyridinic and graphitic −N species whereas cyclic amines and non-graphitic nitrogen containing groups were formed through the Maillard chemistry reaction. The final composition and structure of the optimum N-CDs were further supported by TEM, HRTEM, XPS and FTIR.

The size and surface morphology of the optimum as-made N-CDs sample were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As represented in Fig. 4a and b, TEM images show that the N-CDs are homogeneous and spherical shape with mono-dispersed behavior. HRTEM image shown in Fig. 4c revealed the crystalline nature of the core with a spacing of 0.24 nm, attributing to the value for the (100) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon, which is in agreement with other reports [24–26]. The particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 2d) shows that N-CDs have an average diameter of 4.7 nm (n > 100) based on TEM images.

To gain insight into the surface chemical composition of N-CDs, the optimum sample was analyzed using XPS spectra as presented in Fig. 5 . According to the wide scan XPS (Fig. 5a), the main elemental composition observed in the N-CDs is carbon (61.21%), nitrogen (22.4%) and oxygen (15.69%). The content of nitrogen (22.4%) was higher than that of N-CDs reported in the literature (6.37%, 8.33%) [27,28], indicating that these nanodots were surface passivated by N. The narrow scan of carbon (C1s) was de-convoluted into four carbon bonds (Fig. 5b): CC (284.88 eV), CN (285.82 eV), CO (287.48 eV), and CO (288.17 eV). The existence of CC energy bonds revealed the formation of sp2 domains or graphitic core of N-CDs and meanwhile the signal of CO and CO confirmed the formation of carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots [27]. Three bonding energy peaks at 399.01, 399.8 and 400.76 eV were clearly noticed in nitrogen spectrum (N1s) (Fig. 5c) corresponding to graphitic nitrogen in the form of (NC3), pyridinic N, and NH bands, respectively. The oxygen (O1s) spectrum (Fig. 5d) exhibits two main peaks of OH (530.85 eV) and CO (531.93 eV).

FTIR measurement was further used to assign the presence of containing groups over the surface of N-CDs (Fig. 6 ). The typical absorption peaks at 3373.57, 2149. 79, and 1645.5 cm−1 are assigned to OH, CH, and CH stretching vibrations, respectively [29]. In addition, the peak of NH is detected at 1365.57 cm−1 while stretching vibration of CN appeared at 1217.26 cm−1 [29,30]. Furthermore, the bending vibration of CO and COC appears at 1093.82 and 960.1 cm−1, respectively, confirming the formation of oxidized groups [2,31]. Bending vibration of CH bond corresponding to aromatic features also appears at 764.69 cm−1 [32]. The functional groups detected by FTIR are consistent with the results obtained by XPS survey. The above data confirmed that polar functional groups such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino species were successfully formed on the surface of carbon dots and nitrogen atoms were effectively inserted into the sp2 carbon framework, which impart these nanodots great water solubility, photostability and can be employed for further functionalization and analytical functions.

The optical characterization of the as-made N-CDs dispersed in water is shown in Fig. 7 . Two absorption peaks were observed in the UV region at 274 nm and 298 nm, which were assigned to the π–π* transitions originated from aromatic sp2 hybridized framework and n–π* transition of aromatic CN bonds, respectively in carbon cores [33]. Besides that, there is an absorption edge observed at around 310 nm of visible region and was attributed to the non-bonding electron orbitals such as NH, OH and CO that formed at the surface of N-CDs [33,34]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 7, the water-soluble aqueous solution N-CDs exhibited dark brown dispersion under daylight, while emitted blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. Based on fluorescence spectra, N-CDs exhibited a maximum luminescence at around 426 nm under a maximum excitation of 320 nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) was found to be 96 nm, indicating the suitability of using these nanomaterials in broad range of applications compared to the classical QDs [35].

In order to gain a better insight into the optical behavior of N-CDs, fluorescence study was performed at different excitation wavelengths ranging from (300–420 nm) as presented in Fig. 8 . Most reported literature shown selective excitation luminescence, except several studies that presented excitation in-dependent emission of N-CDs [3,27,36]. This study, however, shows that the as-made N-CDs exhibited both excitation dependent and in-dependent phenomena. The emission intensity was gradually increased with slightly shift centered at 426 ± 3 nm in the excitation wavelength range of 300–320 nm. The fluorescent intensity, however, decreased and gradually red-shifted from 431.5 nm to 497 nm as the excitation increased beyond 320 nm, which is suitable in bioimaging fields [27].

Up to now, the exact PL mechanism of N-CDs is still unclear. However, based on the above data, it can be said that both intrinsic state emission originates from graphitic structure and extrinsic state emission originates from the abundant containing groups over the surface of N-CDs are the main reasons for the PL origin of N-CDs. Normally, when the particle size is small (<5 nm), no notable shift is shown owing to the absence of multi-fluorescence centers since π–π* transition of graphitic carbon cores is the main reason for obtaining emission maxima. However, red-shifted of emission maxima is shown with increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 420 nm, due to the presence of n–π* transition region originated from carbonyl/amino groups in N-CDs as described in the literature reports [37,38].

We developed simple, in-expensive and eco-friendly blue luminescent N-CDs through HTC of EFB carboxymethylcellulose and EDA as both carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. Based on XPS and FTIR results, different containing groups, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups were formed on the surface of N-CDs while pyridinic and graphitic −N were introduced into the sp2 carbon domains. The as-made N-CDs exhibited blue color under UV light, with an optimum QY of 22.9%. Both excitation dependent and in-dependent behaviors of N-CDs were observed. Statistically, response surface methodology shows that quadratic model could be the best model for explaining QY variation with coefficient of determination (R 2 = 0.9991). Good correlation between the experimental and predicted values was obtained. According to ANOVA, temperature has the most synergistic effect for efficient carbonization conversion rate of N-CDs, followed by reaction time and EDA mass. The optimum values of synthetic parameters were as follows: temperature (270 °C), time (6 h) and EDA mass (23.3%).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Universiti Putra Malaysia for funding this project (GP-IPS/2017/9556800).
